title: Diverse mammals use the same tree as their public toilet
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Trippy tobacco, forbidden black holes, and more
Brought to you by the Gairdner Foundation
View this email in your browser
2 April 2026
Today’s Deep Dive examines the bathroom habits of tree-dwelling mammals. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including what happens when stars get too big for their own good and evidence that half of all social science papers can’t be replicated.
plant sciences  |  News from Science
Trippy tobacco? Plants engineered to make five psychedelics at once
“Magic mushrooms” make psilocybin. Tropical plants make the ingredients of the psychoactive ayahuasca. And some toads secrete the mind-altering bufotenin. Now, the tobacco plant makes them all. Thanks to some genetic tinkering, researchers have engineered a close relative of the crop traditionally known for its use in cigarettes to manufacture a wide variety of psychedelics, they report this week in Science Advances.

“This is exciting work,” said bioengineer Andrew Jones, who wasn’t involved in the new study. Although Jones thinks that microbes rather than tobacco will ultimately be used to manufacture psychedelics, he said the insights in the new paper could inform the production of psychoactive medications for severe depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress.

The new study reveals how plants synthesize N,N-dimethyltryptamine (DMT) from the amino acid tryptophan. DMT is the active ingredient in a traditional hallucinogenic drink called ayahuasca, which shamans in the Amazon make by brewing a relative of coffee called chacruna.

The researchers identified two genes—PvTDC2 and PvNMT1—and added them to tobaccolike plants, which then produced DMT. With further changes, the plants made psilocybin and its precursor, as well as bufotenin and 5-methoxy-DMT, hallucinogens secreted by the Sonoran Desert toad.

The team even managed to engineer tobacco plants to produce all five psychedelics at once. “That has some cool novelty, but it doesn’t have a lot of practicality,” Jones said. When made simultaneously, the concentrations were all much smaller. Still, he predicted, “There are a few psychonauts out there that will get a kick out of it.”
Read the Science Advances Paper
Read the full story
Astrophysics  |  news from Science
Go big, then go boom
Gravitational waves given off by distant black hole binaries, such as the one in the background of this artist’s conception, suggest that some stars are too big to form black holes at all—and instead explode as pair instability supernovae.  carl knox/ozgraV/swinburne university of technology
When they die, the universe’s largest stars either embrace the darkness or violently reject it. Some massive stars collapse into themselves when they’ve run out of fuel and transform into black holes. But in the 1960s, physicists first theorized that especially massive stars could swap this fate for another, by exploding at the end of their lives with such ferocity that they completely rip themselves apart. That prediction led to a decades-long mystery: How big must stars be to self-destruct when they die?

The biggest stars are so rare and short-lived, though, that it’s been difficult to prove that beyond a defined stellar mass, there lies a “forbidden range” in which stars are simply too gargantuan to become black holes. Now, astronomers have finally collected enough data to put some numbers on the threshold masses—starting at about 44 times the mass of the Sun. The new study, published yesterday in Nature, provides the strongest evidence yet in support of the theory by carefully analyzing gravitational waves: faint ripples in spacetime created by pairs of distant black holes long ago.

The range of masses that this paper provides “is an incredibly interesting prediction at the interface of particle physics, nuclear physics, stellar evolution, and gravitational wave astronomy,” said Djuna Croon, an astroparticle physicist who was not involved with the new work.
Read the Full Story
social sciences  |  News from Science
Half of social science findings can’t be replicated
With scientific credibility in the hot seat, it is more important than ever that a research study’s results hold up to scrutiny. Now, a sweeping project involving hundreds of researchers has showed that across the social sciences, the findings of roughly half of all papers cannot be replicated independently—and there’s no reliable way to tell in advance which ones will falter.

The Systematizing Confidence in Open Research and Evidence (SCORE) project, the largest of its kind, investigated papers published in dozens of leading journals in business, economics, education, political science, psychology, and sociology. The replication success rate—49% for the 164 papers evaluated, reported yesterday in Nature —is consistent with findings from previous studies in individual fields such as psychology. The team also found similarly low rates on two related measures of what it calls “repeatability.”

There may be no easy fix: The SCORE team hoped to identify key signatures that could be tied to a paper’s credibility and perhaps harnessed to give readers a metric for how confident they can be about the reported results, but no marker stood out as reliable. “A lot more evidence is needed before we would be confident in a valid, scalable solution,” said psychologist Brian Nosek, a SCORE project leader.

A paper’s failure to be replicated does not necessarily mean its findings are wrong, Nosek added, but that it warrants scrutiny—especially when public policy or human welfare is directly riding on the result. The failure provides “new evidence, which then prompts additional questions, additional exploring, and eventually we get to answers we can be confident in.”
Read the Full Story
SPONSORED
2026 Canada Gairdner Awards Honour Breakthrough Science
Meet the 2026 laureates recognized for groundbreaking discoveries advancing human health, from fundamental biology to global health research, with advances that are transforming our understanding of disease and shaping the future of medicine.
Read Now
Deep Dive
The margay, a small tree-dwelling wildcat, is notoriously difficult to photograph in the wild. This one has been caught with its (metaphorical) pants down.  tropical canopy ecology project
When nature calls, diverse tree-dwelling mammals seek out the same species
All animals, regardless of habitat, must heed the call of nature. But where do wild critters go when they need to go?

Some terrestrial animals do their business in communal latrines—more rugged versions of a public bathroom. These sites can serve as communication hubs among and between species and often play an important role in shaping local ecosystems. Raccoon latrines, for example, may create an “ecology of fear” that scares off other species at risk of dying from roundworm parasites in racoon poop.

Scientists know comparatively little, meanwhile, about the habits of animals that dwell in forest canopies. Learning more would involve scaling large trees, which presents a number of dangers for researchers. These obstacles are no match, however, for ecologist Jeremy Quirós-Navarro, who recently spent years climbing hundreds of trees in the cloud forests of Costa Rica. He knows how to spot rotten branches and other hazards, sometimes jumping from one tree to its neighbor and relying on multiple ropes to minimize risk. He takes care not to disturb the venomous snakes that inhabit the canopy, and so far he has managed to avoid the infamously painful sting of the bullet ant.

One day, while Quirós-Navarro was climbing a strangler fig (Ficus tuerckheimii), he came across a natural platform covered in many different types of animal feces. The discovery wasn’t terribly surprising by itself, but as Quirós-Navarro continued climbing, he kept finding more latrines—all located on the same tree species. “It was definitely a pattern,” he says.
Ecologist Jeremy Quirós-Navarro climbs next to one of the latrine sites he has studied.  tropical canopy ecology project
Quirós-Navarro and his colleagues decided to set up a camera trap at one latrine, which was located in the Monteverde Cloud Forest Preserve. As the team reported last month in Ecology and Evolution, the camera captured visits from an astonishing 17 mammal species , including porcupines, coatis, and several monkeys. “I was in shock,” Quirós-Navarro recalls. “It’s basically almost all the canopy mammals.” One of the most surprising visitors was the two-toed sloth, an animal long thought to only defecate on the ground. Quirós-Navarro was also excited to catch a glimpse of the elusive margay—a small wildcat he had spent years trying to photograph.

It remains unclear why all these mammals seem to gravitate toward strangler figs, but Quirós-Navarro has a few theories. These trees, he notes, are particularly large, with thick horizontal branches that form connections with neighboring trees. Latrines appear to be more common in strangler figs located next to rivers, which suggests that canopy-dwelling mammals are using them as bridges between different parts of the forest.

In the future, Quirós-Navarro hopes to investigate how these latrines may be impacting the cycling of nutrients in the canopy and determine what role they serve in interactions between animals. “This could be a keystone species for mammal communication,” he says. This research is especially important, he adds, because strangler figs are a major attraction for tourists, who frequently camp out in their branches—activities that could be disrupting latrines (not to mention making for some smelly campsites).
Read the Paper
Et Cetera
Out for blood
A blood test for the p-tau217 biomarker can help diagnose Alzheimer’s disease, but some scientists fear overuse will be used to push healthy people toward unnecessary drugs.
Read more at News from Science
What’s in a name?
Growing evidence for a “male version” of polycystic ovary syndrome lends more support to the idea that the condition’s current name is misleading.
Read more at STAT
Fish out of water
A warty frogfish named Domino, the sole survivor among tens of thousands of eggs laid by its mother, is the first of its kind to be raised in captivity.
Read more at NPR
Investing in newborn care is both a moral and economic imperative.
—Alice Tarus, the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine
SCIENCEINSIDER  |  1 april 2026  |  abdullahi tsanni
More than 60 countries are off track to meet a U.N. goal to reduce infant deaths by 2030. Although solutions exist, dramatic cuts to global aid threaten to undo progress.
Last but not least
This 500-million-year-old fossil reveals some seriously cool details about the oldest known relatives of spiders and scorpions. That said, I would probably still scream if I found one in the shower.
Phie Jacobs, General Assignment Reporter, Science

With contributions from Erik Stokstad, Perri Thaler, and Jeffrey Brainard
